local_names.romanized_local: Yīng Zuǐ Dòu (Chinese); Kondai Kadalai (Tamil); Chane / Chole (Hindi)

food_subgroup: Cooked legumes — chickpeas (garbanzo beans) boiled from dried; 1 cup = 164g cooked

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for cooked chickpeas as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 1 cup (164g) of chickpeas cooked from dried, equivalent to approximately 269 kcal with 14.6g protein, 12.5g dietary fibre, and 4.3g fat.

nutrient_source.title: Chickpeas (garbanzo beans, bengal gram), mature seeds, cooked, boiled, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 173756)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced from USDA SR Legacy for cooked chickpeas (boiled from dried, without salt). All per-100g values match USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173756 within rounding tolerance.

gi_source.title: Chickpeas, boiled — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Research Service)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI = 28 (low) for chickpeas cooked from dried, as reported in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (28 × 44.9g per 164g serving) / 100 ≈ 12.6.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (7mg/100g; 11mg per cup) with high potassium (291mg/100g; 477mg per cup) and magnesium (48mg/100g; 79mg per cup)
hypertension.guidance: Chickpeas are an excellent choice for hypertension management: their very low sodium content (11mg per cup when cooked without salt), combined with potassium (477mg per cup, ~14% RDA) and magnesium (79mg per cup, ~20% RDA), supports blood pressure reduction and aligns with DASH dietary guidelines; high soluble fibre also contributes to LDL-C reduction and overall cardiovascular risk reduction.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic index (GI 28) and high dietary fibre (7.6g/100g; 12.5g per cup) with significant protein content attenuating postprandial glucose response
type2_diabetes.guidance: Chickpeas are one of the most evidence-based foods for Type 2 Diabetes management: GI ~28 and GL ~12.6 per cup produce minimal postprandial glucose excursion; the combination of resistant starch, soluble fibre, and protein slows gastric emptying and glucose absorption; colonic fermentation of chickpea fibre produces short-chain fatty acids that improve insulin sensitivity at subsequent meals (the 'second meal effect'), making chickpeas a preferred carbohydrate source for glycaemic control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High soluble dietary fibre (7.6g/100g), very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g), zero cholesterol, and significant potassium and folate content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Chickpeas support cardiovascular health through multiple mechanisms: high soluble fibre reduces LDL cholesterol; very low saturated fat (0.3g/100g) and zero cholesterol minimise atherogenic lipid burden; high folate (172mcg/100g) helps lower homocysteine levels, a cardiovascular risk factor; potassium supports vascular tone; and the overall low energy density and high satiety value support healthy weight management, reducing overall CVD risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High folate (172mcg/100g; 282mcg per cup) complementing methotrexate therapy, plus iron (2.9mg/100g) addressing anaemia of chronic disease
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: For rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate, chickpeas provide clinically relevant dietary folate (282mcg DFE per cup, ~71% RDA) that complements standard folate supplementation; non-haem iron (4.7mg per cup) supports iron stores in patients with anaemia of chronic disease; modest anti-inflammatory effects from magnesium and phytoestrogens provide additional benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Chickpeas (also known as garbanzo beans or Bengal gram; Cicer arietinum) are a legume cooked from dried seeds by boiling, yielding a firm yet creamy-textured bean with a mild, nutty, slightly earthy flavour. One standard serving is 1 cup cooked (164g), providing approximately 269 kcal, 14.6g protein, 4.3g fat, 44.9g carbohydrate, 12.5g dietary fibre, and only 11mg sodium. Chickpeas have a glycemic index of approximately 28 (low) and a glycemic load of ~12.6 per cup, making them one of the lowest-GI carbohydrate foods available. The combination of resistant starch, soluble fibre, and plant protein creates a sustained satiety response with minimal postprandial glucose excursion. The 'second meal effect' — colonic fermentation of chickpea fibre producing short-chain fatty acids that improve insulin sensitivity at subsequent meals — is a clinically documented metabolic benefit unique to legumes. Chickpeas are exceptionally rich in folate (172mcg/100g; 282mcg per cup, ~71% RDA), iron (2.9mg/100g; 4.7mg per cup), potassium (291mg/100g; 477mg per cup), magnesium (48mg/100g), phosphorus (168mg/100g), and zinc (1.5mg/100g). They are naturally cholesterol-free, very low in saturated fat (0.3g/100g), and gluten-free. Chickpeas are a staple of Indian (chole curry, dal, chaat), Middle Eastern (hummus, falafel), and Mediterranean cuisines, and are widely available in Singapore canned (NTUC FairPrice) or dried. They are suitable for vegetarian, vegan, halal, and gluten-free diets. Clinically, chickpeas are among the most evidence-based foods for Type 2 Diabetes management, hypertension (DASH diet), cardiovascular disease prevention, and for rheumatoid arthritis patients on methotrexate requiring dietary folate support.

confidence_reason: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from USDA SR Legacy (FDC ID 173756) for cooked chickpeas boiled from dried without salt — an exact match to the food described. GI value of 28 sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for chickpeas cooked from dried. Ontology codes (FoodOn FOODON_03301075 and FoodEx2 A00XB) are defensible direct matches. All mandatory multilingual names for this internationally consumed legume are present.
notes: Cholesterol is confirmed zero (0.0mg/100g) per USDA SR Legacy — chickpeas are a plant food and naturally contain no cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is confirmed zero (0.0mcg/100g) — naturally zero as chickpeas are a plant food containing no vitamin B12. Vitamin D is confirmed zero (0.0mcg/100g) — naturally zero as chickpeas are a plant food with no meaningful vitamin D content. Trans fat is confirmed zero (0.0g/100g) per USDA SR Legacy. Monounsaturated fat (0.6g/100g) and polyunsaturated fat (1.2g/100g) values sourced from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 173756. Water content (60.2g/100g) sourced from USDA SR Legacy. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI 28 × 44.9g available carbohydrate per 164g serving) / 100 = 12.6. The food_subgroup in the input was truncated; full description restored. The input food_group 'Whole Grains & Legumes' has been mapped to 'Meat and alternatives' per standard food group taxonomy for legumes used as protein sources. SNOMED CT code not available for cooked chickpeas as a food product; FoodOn and FoodEx2 anchors are sufficient for ontological alignment.
